papers.title: Directional Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants
papers.why_relevant: Its directional coarea, boundary-pushing, and filling estimates in rectangles are well suited to preserving anisotropic side-length exponents in the source-side filling argument.

lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
lemmas.statement: Directional filling of absolute cycles in a rectangle. Let R = [0,R_1] √ó ... √ó [0,R_n] be a Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R_1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ R_n. For an integral Lipschitz chain C in R^n and a coordinate tuple A of cardinality dim C, write Vol_A(C) for the mass of the orthogonal projection of C to the coordinate plane spanned by the axes in A, counted with geometric multiplicity, equivalently the integral over C of the absolute value of the corresponding coordinate component of the unit tangent multivector. If z is an integral Lipschitz m-cycle in R, with 0 ‚â§ m < n and ‚àÇz = 0, then there is an integral Lipschitz (m+1)-chain y in R with ‚àÇy = z such that for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, if i is the smallest element of I and I‚àñ{i} denotes the m-tuple obtained by deleting i, then Vol_I(y) ‚â§ R_i Vol_{I‚àñ{i}}(z).
lemmas.proof_sketch: Induct on n. Let œÄ be orthogonal projection of R to the face x_1 = 0. There is a straight-line homology H from z to œÄ(z), obtained by sliding each point parallel to the x_1-axis. Its only nonzero directional volumes are those involving direction 1: for I = {1}‚à™J, Vol_I(H) ‚â§ R_1 Vol_J(z), and if 1 is not in I then Vol_I(H)=0. Since z is a cycle, œÄ(z) is an m-cycle in the (n‚àí1)-rectangle [0,R_2]√ó...√ó[0,R_n]. By the induction hypothesis, œÄ(z) bounds an (m+1)-chain y‚Ä≤ in that face with Vol_I(y‚Ä≤) ‚â§ R_i Vol_{I‚àñ{i}}(œÄ(z)) ‚â§ R_i Vol_{I‚àñ{i}}(z) for every I not containing 1; for I containing 1, y‚Ä≤ has zero I-volume because it lies in x_1=0. Then y = H + y‚Ä≤ has boundary z and satisfies the asserted estimate. The ordering R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n is used only to identify the smallest coordinate in I as the side length appearing in the bound; no loss occurs in the projection because coordinate projections are 1-Lipschitz on directional volumes.

lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
lemmas.statement: Directional boundary-pushing for relative cycles in a rectangle. Let R = [0,R_1] √ó ... √ó [0,R_n] be a Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R_1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ R_n, and define directional volumes Vol_A as in Proposition 2.1. Let z be an integral Lipschitz m-chain in R which is a relative cycle, meaning ‚àÇz is supported in ‚àÇR. Then there exist an integral Lipschitz (m+1)-chain y in R and an integral Lipschitz m-chain B supported in ‚àÇR such that ‚àÇy = z + B, with the following bounds. First, for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let e be the smallest coordinate index not in I, using the harmless convention e=n+1 if I={1,...,n}; then Vol_I(y) ‚â§ C(n) sum_{d=1}^{e‚àí1} R_d Vol_{I‚àñ{d}}(z). Second, for every coordinate m-tuple J not containing 1, let e be the smallest index in {2,...,n} not in J, again using e=n+1 if J contains all of {2,...,n}; then Vol_J(B) ‚â§ C(n)[ Vol_J(z) + sum_{d=1}^{e‚àí1} R_1^{-1} R_d Vol_{({1}‚à™J)‚àñ{d}}(z) ]. Here C(n) depends only on n, and all tuple-deletion expressions in the sums are well-defined by the definition of e.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n, with a special sliding lemma as the basic move. If ‚àÇz avoids the top face x_1=R_1, slide z to the bottom face by f(x,t)=(t x_1,x_2,...,x_n). The swept chain y has ‚àÇy=z+B with B‚äÇ‚àÇR. It has Vol_I(y)‚â§R_1 Vol_{I‚àñ{1}}(z) when 1‚ààI and zero I-volume otherwise. For J not containing 1, the only part of B contributing to Vol_J is the bottom projection of z, so Vol_J(B)‚â§Vol_J(z). For a general relative cycle choose a height h by the coarea inequality so that every directional slice z_h=z‚à©{x_1=h} satisfies Vol_K(z_h)‚â§C R_1^{-1}Vol_{{1}‚à™K}(z). Decompose z as z_1+z_2 with z_2=[0,R_1]√óz_h and z_1=z‚àíz_2. Split z_1 into upper and lower pieces; each avoids one of the top or bottom faces, so the sliding lemma applies. The slice choice controls the extra cylinder [0,R_1]√óz_h, giving chains y_1,B_1 satisfying the stated estimates. For z_2, apply the induction hypothesis in the (n‚àí1)-rectangle [0,R_2]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] to z_h, obtaining y_h with ‚àÇy_h=z_h+B_h and directional estimates. Set y_2=[0,R_1]√óy_h. Then ‚àÇy_2=z_2+B_2, where B_2=[0,R_1]√óB_h‚àí{0}√óy_h+{R_1}√óy_h lies in ‚àÇR. If I contains 1, Vol_I(y_2)=R_1 Vol_{I‚àñ{1}}(y_h), and induction plus the slice bounds gives the required sum; if I omits 1, the I-volume is zero. For J omitting 1, the side cylinder [0,R_1]√óB_h has zero J-volume, while the two end copies contribute Vol_J(y_h), again controlled by induction and the slice estimate. Adding y=y_1+y_2 and B=B_1+B_2 proves the proposition.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
lemmas.statement: Directional coarea inequality. Let y be a smooth m-chain in R^n, let (N^q,h) be a q-dimensional Riemannian manifold of finite positive volume, and let F:y‚ÜíN be a smooth map whose q-dilation is at most Œõ, i.e. the operator norm of the induced map on q-vectors is everywhere at most Œõ. Put k=m‚àíq, assume k‚â•0, and define directional volumes Vol_A for chains in R^n as in Proposition 2.1. Then there is a fiber z=F^{-1}(p), for some regular value p‚ààN, such that for every coordinate k-tuple J, Vol_J(z) ‚â§ C(n) Œõ Vol(N,h)^{-1} sum_{I‚äÉJ, |I|=m} Vol_I(y). The sum is over all coordinate m-tuples I containing J, and C(n) depends only on n.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the general coarea formula with a carefully chosen weight. At a point x where dF has full rank q, the kernel of dF restricted to T_x y is a k-plane; let V(F)(x) be its unit k-vector. For a fixed coordinate k-tuple J, use the weight G(x)=|J¬∑V(F)(x)| in the coarea formula. The left side becomes ‚à´_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp. The right side is ‚à´_y Jac_q(dF) |J¬∑V(F)| dvol, which is at most Œõ‚à´_y |J¬∑V(F)| because the q-Jacobian is bounded by the q-dilation. The key linear algebra estimate is: for any m-plane P‚äÇR^n, sup over k-planes Q‚äÇP of |J¬∑Q| equals (sum_{I‚äÉJ}|I¬∑P|^2)^{1/2}. To prove it, rotate P by an orthogonal map fixing the coordinate plane J so that P‚à©J^‚ä• is a coordinate (m‚àík)-plane K. Writing P=Q_0‚äïK, any Q‚äÇP has |J¬∑Q|‚â§|J¬∑Q_0|, and the only nonzero term on the right is I=J‚à™K, giving equality. Hence |J¬∑V(F)(x)|‚â§sum_{I‚äÉJ}|I¬∑T_x y|. Integrating gives ‚à´_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp ‚â§ Œõ sum_{I‚äÉJ}Vol_I(y). Since there are only finitely many J, choose one regular value p for which all these averaged inequalities hold simultaneously, losing only a binomial constant absorbed into C(n).

papers.title: Area-Contracting Maps Between Rectangles
papers.why_relevant: This is the closest source: Chapter 8 contains the relative filling-volume estimates for coordinate planes and cheap fillings of small relative 2-cycles in rectangles, including the solid-torus mechanism behind the mixed factor S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}.

lemmas.label: Proposition 8.1.1
lemmas.statement: Let E and F be 3-dimensional ellipsoids in Euclidean 4-space, with principal axes R_1 ‚â§ R_2 ‚â§ R_3 ‚â§ R_4 and S_1 ‚â§ S_2 ‚â§ S_3 ‚â§ S_4, respectively. There are positive universal constants c and C with the following properties. If there is a diffeomorphism f:E‚ÜíF with 2-dilation at most 1, then the following lower bounds hold: (B1) R_2 R_3 ‚â• c S_2 S_3; (B2) R_2^2 R_3 R_4 ‚â• c S_2^2 S_3 S_4; (B3a) if R_2^2 ‚â• S_2 S_3, then R_2 R_3 R_4 ‚â• c S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4; and (B3b) if R_2^2 ‚â§ S_2 S_3, then R_3 R_4 ‚â• c S_3 S_4. Conversely, if the corresponding inequalities hold with a sufficiently large constant in place of the small constant, Guth constructs a diffeomorphism E‚ÜíF whose 2-dilation is bounded by a universal constant.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has three independent lower-bound mechanisms. For (B1), the 2-width of a 3-dimensional ellipsoid with axes R_1‚â§R_2‚â§R_3‚â§R_4 is comparable to R_2R_3: the upper bound comes from slicing by the long coordinate, and the lower bound from a rectangular region quasi-isometric to R_2√óR_3√óR_4. A 2-contracting diffeomorphism cannot decrease the target 2-width when pulled back, so R_2R_3‚â≥S_2S_3. For (B2), pack F by about N‚âàS_3S_4/S_2^2 disjoint subsets each quasi-isometric to an S_2-cube. Each has 2-width ‚â≥S_2^2, hence each inverse image in E has 2-width ‚â≥S_2^2. The width-volume inequality inside E gives volume at least ‚â≥S_2^4/R_2 for each inverse image. Since the inverse images are disjoint and Vol(E)‚âàR_2R_3R_4, one gets R_2R_3R_4‚â≥N S_2^4/R_2, i.e. R_2^2R_3R_4‚â≥S_2^2S_3S_4. For (B3), place in F a solid torus modeled on a family of circles of length scale S_2 parametrized over a central S_3√óS_4 rectangle. Each circle has filling area at least ‚â≥S_2S_3 in F. Pulling back by the diffeomorphism, the transverse 2-area to the circle family cannot decrease, because f is 2-contracting. Also, if a curve in E has length L<cR_2, it lies in a uniformly Euclidean ball and fills with area ‚â§CL^2. Thus if R_2^2‚â•S_2S_3, each pulled-back circle has length ‚â≥(S_2S_3)^{1/2}, giving preimage solid-torus volume ‚â≥S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4, and hence R_2R_3R_4‚â≥that quantity. If R_2^2‚â§S_2S_3, the same filling-area obstruction forces each pulled-back circle to have length ‚â≥R_2, so the preimage has volume ‚â≥R_2S_3S_4; comparing with Vol(E)‚âàR_2R_3R_4 gives R_3R_4‚â≥S_3S_4. The converse uses explicit non-linear constructions: snake-map/quasi-isometric foldings and simple linear rescalings, with separate cases according to whether R_2^2 is above or below S_2S_3.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2.1
lemmas.statement: Let R be an n-dimensional rectangle with side lengths R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n, and let U be an open subset of R. For 0‚â§l‚â§k<n, the k-width of U satisfies W_k(U) ‚â§ C(n) (R_1...R_l)^{(n-k)/(n-l)} Vol(U)^{(k-l)/(n-l)}. Here W_k(U) is the infimum of W such that there is a generic piecewise-linear map œÄ:U‚ÜíR^{n-k} all of whose fibers have k-dimensional volume at most W. The case l=0 is the width-volume inequality W_k(U)‚â§C(n)Vol(U)^{k/n}, while the case l=k gives the trivial rectangle bound W_k(U)‚â§C(n)R_1...R_k.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint l=0 is Theorem 3.1: for a compactly supported continuous function 0‚â§f‚â§1 on R^n, one builds a generic PL projection whose fibers have f-mass ‚â§C(‚à´f)^{k/n}; approximating the characteristic function of U gives W_k(U)‚â§CVol(U)^{k/n}. The endpoint l=k follows because U‚äÇR and projection to the last n‚àík coordinates has fibers contained in k-rectangles of volume R_1...R_k. For 0<l<k, approximate 1_U from above by a continuous f supported near U with 0‚â§f‚â§1 and ‚à´f‚âàVol(U). Average f over the first l coordinates: g(y)=(R_1...R_l)^{-1}‚à´_{[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_l]} f(x,y) dx, where y lies in the (n‚àíl)-rectangle R_{l+1}√ó...√óR_n. Then 0‚â§g‚â§1 and ‚à´g‚âàVol(U)/(R_1...R_l). Apply the width-volume inequality in dimension n‚àíl to g with width dimension k‚àíl, obtaining a generic PL map œÄ_0 in the y-variables whose fibers have g-mass ‚â§C(Vol(U)/(R_1...R_l))^{(k-l)/(n-l)}. Lift it to U by œÄ(x,y)=œÄ_0(y). Each œÄ-fiber is the product of an l-rectangle of volume R_1...R_l with a œÄ_0-fiber, so its f-mass, hence the volume of U in that fiber, is at most (R_1...R_l) times the preceding bound. Rearranging gives the stated exponent: C(R_1...R_l)^{(n-k)/(n-l)}Vol(U)^{(k-l)/(n-l)}.

lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
lemmas.statement: Let U be a bounded open subset of R^n with smooth boundary. Then ‚àÇU contains pairwise disjoint subsets S_i such that Vol(U) ‚â§ C(n) Œ£_i W_{n-2}(S_i)^{n/(n-2)}, where W_{n-2} denotes (n‚àí2)-width. In particular, in dimension n=4 this reads Vol(U) ‚â§ C Œ£_i W_2(S_i)^2.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Call a ball B(p,R) good if W_{n-2}(‚àÇU‚à©B(p,R))‚â•cR^{n-2}. The first step is to show that for every p‚ààU some radius R gives a good ball. For each R define Œ¶_R:R^n‚ÜíS^n by mapping B(p,R) to the complement of the south pole via the exponential map and collapsing the complement of B(p,R) to the south pole. On ‚àÇU√ó(0,‚àû), put a metric g that on the slice ‚àÇU√ó{R} is essentially (œÄ/R)^2 times the original metric on ‚àÇU‚à©B(p,R), very small outside that ball, and very large in the R-direction. Then Œ¶(x,R)=Œ¶_R(x) is contracting. For large A, after a harmless pinch near the poles, Œ¶ gives a degree-one map from (‚àÇU√ó[A^{-1},A],g) to the unit S^n, sending each boundary component to a point. Gromov's width lower bound for a nonzero-degree map to S^n gives W_{n-2}(‚àÇU√ó[A^{-1},A],g)‚â•c. The slicing lemma says that for a metric on M√ó[0,1], W_{m-1}(M√ó[0,1])‚â§2 sup_t W_{m-1}(M,g_t). Applying it with M=‚àÇU yields some R for which the slice ‚àÇU√ó{R}, hence ‚àÇU‚à©B(p,R) after rescaling by R, has (n‚àí2)-width ‚â•cR^{n-2}. Thus every p‚ààU is covered by a good ball. The Vitali covering lemma selects pairwise disjoint good balls B(p_i,R_i) with Œ£_i R_i^n ‚â• c Vol(U). Setting S_i=‚àÇU‚à©B(p_i,R_i), goodness gives W_{n-2}(S_i)^{n/(n-2)} ‚â• cR_i^n. Summing over i gives the theorem.

papers.title: The Width-Volume Inequality
papers.why_relevant: Provides the baseline width-volume inequalities for k-dilation maps and the singular-value monotonicity step used to pass from Dil_2 <= 1 to control of higher-dimensional fillings.

lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2
lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R1] √ó ... √ó [0,Rn] be an n-dimensional Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ Rn, and let U be an open subset of R. For 0 ‚â§ l ‚â§ k < n, let W_k(U) denote the k-width of U, i.e. the infimum, over all families of relative flat k-cycles sweeping out U, of the supremal k-mass of a cycle in the family. Then there is a constant C(n), depending only on n, such that W_k(U) < C(n) (R1...Rl)^((n‚àík)/(n‚àíl)) Vol_n(U)^((k‚àíl)/(n‚àíl)). The empty product for l=0 is interpreted as 1.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint l=0 is the width-volume inequality. After scaling to Vol(U)=1, translate the unit cubical lattice so that its k-skeleton S meets U in k-measure O_n(1), by averaging over translations. Construct a degree-one proper PL map Œ® of R^n which collapses most of space to S: good simplices map into S, while bad simplices lie in a small neighborhood of the dual (n‚àík‚àí1)-skeleton. Applying Œ® to a generic family of parallel k-planes gives a sweepout; genericity ensures each plane meets only O_n(1) bad simplices, so each image cycle lies in S with bounded multiplicity except for O_n(1) additional k-mass. Restricting to U gives mass O_n(1), and rescaling gives W_k(U) ‚â§ C Vol(U)^{k/n}. The endpoint l=k follows from monotonicity W_k(U) ‚â§ W_k(R) and the standard sweepout of R by k-rectangles of volume R1...Rk. For intermediate l, define a function f on the (n‚àíl)-rectangle [0,R_{l+1}]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] by f(y)=(R1...Rl)^{-1} Vol_l(U ‚à© ([0,R1]√ó...√ó[0,Rl]√ó{y})). Then 0‚â§f‚â§1 and ‚à´f=Vol(U)/(R1...Rl). Apply the function version of the width-volume inequality in dimension n‚àíl to get a family F of (k‚àíl)-cycles with ‚à´_{F(p)} f ‚â§ C(‚à´f)^{(k‚àíl)/(n‚àíl)}. Taking products F(p)√ó[0,R1]√ó...√ó[0,Rl] gives k-cycles sweeping out the corresponding product region; restricting them to U yields k-mass at most (R1...Rl) C(Vol(U)/(R1...Rl))^{(k‚àíl)/(n‚àíl)}, which is exactly the displayed bound.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
lemmas.statement: Let f be a piecewise smooth map between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds. Suppose that its k-dilation satisfies Dil_k(f) ‚â§ Œª for some 1 ‚â§ k < n. Then for every integer i with 1 ‚â§ i ‚â§ n‚àík, the (k+i)-dilation satisfies Dil_{k+i}(f) ‚â§ Œª^((k+i)/k). In particular, if Dil_k(f) ‚â§ 1, then Dil_j(f) ‚â§ 1 for every j ‚â• k.
lemmas.proof_sketch: At each differentiability point, let 0 ‚â§ s1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ sn be the singular values of df. By the exterior-power characterization of dilation, the pointwise k-Jacobian norm is s_{n‚àík+1}...s_n, and the pointwise (k+i)-Jacobian norm is s_{n‚àík‚àíi+1}...s_n. Since s_{n‚àík+1} is the smallest of the largest k singular values, s_{n‚àík+1}^k ‚â§ s_{n‚àík+1}...s_n ‚â§ Œª. The extra i factors in the (k+i)-fold product are all at most s_{n‚àík+1}, hence s_{n‚àík‚àíi+1}...s_n ‚â§ s_{n‚àík+1}^i (s_{n‚àík+1}...s_n) ‚â§ Œª^{i/k} Œª = Œª^{(k+i)/k}. Taking the supremum over all differentiability points gives the claimed dilation bound; the nonsmooth locus of a piecewise smooth map is irrelevant for the supremum over smooth pieces.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional Euclidean rectangles with side lengths R1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ Rn and S1 ‚â§ ... ‚â§ Sn, respectively, and put Q_i = S_i/R_i. Let U be a bounded open subset of R for which degree-one maps of pairs (U,‚àÇU) ‚Üí (S,‚àÇS) are defined. Let D_k(U,S) be the infimum of the k-dilation over all such degree-one maps. For every 1 ‚â§ k < n and every integer l with 0 ‚â§ l ‚â§ k, there is a constant c(n)>0 depending only on n such that D_k(U,S) ‚â• c(n) Q1...Ql (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^((k‚àíl)/(n‚àíl)). The empty product Q1...Ql is interpreted as 1 when l=0.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Introduce the packing width P_{k,N}(X): the supremal W such that X contains N disjoint open subsets each having k-width at least W. If f:U‚ÜíS has degree 1 and k-dilation Œª, and S_i are N disjoint subsets of S, then U_i=f^{-1}(S_i) are disjoint in U and, by excision/naturality of degree, f restricts to a nonzero-degree map (U_i,‚àÇU_i)‚Üí(S_i,‚àÇS_i). Monotonicity of k-width under maps with k-dilation Œª gives W_k(U_i) ‚â• W_k(S_i)/Œª. Thus Œª P_{k,N}(U) ‚â• P_{k,N}(S). Since U‚äÇR, P_{k,N}(U) ‚â§ P_{k,N}(R), so Œª ‚â• P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R) for every N. The needed estimates for P_{k,N} are obtained in Proposition 2.3: for a rectangle A with sides A1‚â§...‚â§An, P_{k,N}(A) is, up to C(n), the minimum over 0‚â§j‚â§k of A1...Aj (A_{j+1}...A_n)^((k‚àíj)/(n‚àíj)) N^{‚àí(k‚àíj)/(n‚àíj)}. The upper bound uses Proposition 2.2 on one of N disjoint sets of volume at most Vol(A)/N; the lower bound comes from cutting A into a rectangular grid of N subrectangles of dimensions A1√ó...√óAj√óL√ó...√óL. Choosing N at the scale for which the l-th grid term is realized in S, e.g. N‚âàS_{l+1}...S_n/S_l^{n‚àíl} for 1‚â§l‚â§k with endpoint modifications for l=0,k, gives P_{k,N}(S) ‚â• c S1...Sl S_l^{k‚àíl}, while P_{k,N}(R) is bounded above by the corresponding l-term. Algebra yields P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R) ‚â• c Q1...Ql(Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^((k‚àíl)/(n‚àíl)). Hence every degree-one f has at least this k-dilation. The paper also notes an additional elementary lower bound in the special case k=n‚àí1: if f:R‚ÜíS has (n‚àí1)-dilation Œª, then f|‚àÇR is surjective, so Œª Vol_{n‚àí1}(‚àÇR) ‚â• Vol_{n‚àí1}(‚àÇS); since Vol(‚àÇR)‚â§2n R2...Rn and Vol(‚àÇS)‚â•2 S2...Sn, one gets Œª ‚â• c(n) Q2...Qn.

papers.title: Minimax Problems Related to Cup Powers and Steenrod Squares
papers.why_relevant: Gives a rigorous cycle-space framework, including gluing homomorphisms and minimax comparison under degree-one k-dilation maps, useful for formalizing fiber families and higher-order filling obstructions.

lemmas.label: Section 1: Almgren gluing homomorphism and the fundamental cohomology class a(k,n)
lemmas.statement: Let B^n be the closed unit n-ball and let Z(k,n) be the metric completion, in the area-distance/flat topology, of mod-2 relative Lipschitz k-cycles in (B^n,‚àÇB^n). If X is a simplicial complex and F:X‚ÜíZ(k,n) is a continuous family of k-cycles, then F canonically determines a gluing homomorphism G_F:H_i(X;Z2)‚ÜíH_{k+i}(B^n,‚àÇB^n;Z2) for every i. Concretely, after choosing a sufficiently fine triangulation of X and a sufficiently small parameter Œ¥, one may choose for every i-face A of X a relative mod-2 Lipschitz (k+i)-chain C(A) such that whenever ‚àÇA=Œ£ B_j, one has ‚àÇC(A)=Œ£ C(B_j), and such that at vertices v the cycle C(v) is Œ¥-close to F(v). This chain map induces G_F. The induced homomorphism is independent of all choices, is unchanged by refining the triangulation, and is homotopy invariant in F. In particular, applying this construction to cycles in the space Z(k,n) itself gives a homomorphism H_{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2)‚ÜíH_n(B^n,‚àÇB^n;Z2)=Z2; by the universal coefficient theorem this homomorphism is represented by a canonical class a(k,n)‚ààH^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2). For every family F:X‚ÜíZ(k,n) and every h‚ààH_{n-k}(X;Z2), the pairing ‚ü®F^*a(k,n),h‚ü© is exactly G_F(h). Thus F detects a(k,n) precisely when its gluing homomorphism in degree n‚àík is nonzero, i.e. when the family sweeps out the ball mod 2. For the standard family of parallel k-planes R^k√ó{t}, t‚àà[-1,1]^{n-k}, restricted to B^n and with boundary parameters sent to the empty relative cycle, the pullback of a(k,n) is the fundamental cohomology class of ([-1,1]^{n-k},‚àÇ[-1,1]^{n-k}).
lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a fine triangulation of X. At each vertex v choose a Lipschitz relative k-cycle C(v) close to F(v). If v0,v1 span an edge, continuity of F and fineness of the triangulation make C(v0)‚àíC(v1) small in area-distance, so choose a small (k+1)-chain filling it. Inductively, suppose C has been chosen on the boundary of an i-simplex Œî. The boundary Œ£ C(‚àÇŒî) is a relative (k+i‚àí1)-cycle of very small volume. For k+i‚àí1<n, fill it by Federer-Fleming isoperimetry; for dimension n, small volume forces degree zero and hence fillability; above dimension n fillability is automatic in this relative chain complex. This gives a chain map from simplicial chains of X to relative Lipschitz chains in B^n, shifted by k, hence a homology homomorphism G_F. If two choices, two Œ¥'s, or a refinement are used, build a chain map on X√ó[0,1] interpolating between them; the same inductive filling argument gives a chain homotopy, so G_F is canonical. A homotopy of families is handled identically on X√ó[0,1]. If z and z‚Ä≤ are homologous (n‚àík)-cycles in Z(k,n), glue over a chain with boundary z‚àíz‚Ä≤ to see the resulting n-dimensional relative homology classes in B^n agree; hence the assignment on H_{n-k}(Z(k,n)) is well-defined and yields a cohomology class a(k,n). For parallel planes, take C(Œî)=B^n‚à©(R^k√óŒî); summing over top-dimensional Œî gives the fundamental class of (B^n,‚àÇB^n).

lemmas.label: Propositions 9.1 and 9.2: minimax comparison under degree-one k-dilation maps
lemmas.statement: Let (M,g) and (N,h) be compact n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, using absolute mod-2 k-cycle spaces when the manifolds are closed and relative mod-2 k-cycle spaces for manifolds with boundary. Let a(k,M) and a(k,N) denote their fundamental cohomology classes in degree n‚àík, defined by the gluing construction. For a cohomology class Œ≤ in the relevant cycle space, define V_{(M,g)}(Œ≤)=inf_F sup_{C in F} Vol_k(C), where F ranges over all continuous families of k-cycles detecting Œ≤. Suppose Œ¶:(M,g)‚Üí(N,h), or a map of pairs Œ¶:(M,‚àÇM)‚Üí(N,‚àÇN), is piecewise C^1, has degree 1 mod 2, and has k-dilation at most Œõ, meaning every k-dimensional submanifold has image k-volume at most Œõ times its k-volume. If O is any natural cohomology operation with mod-2 coefficients, for example a cup power or an iterated Steenrod square, then Œõ V_{(M,g)}(O a(k,M)) ‚â• V_{(N,h)}(O a(k,N)). More generally, if U‚äÇM is an open set with piecewise smooth boundary and Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)‚Üí(N,‚àÇN) is piecewise C^1, degree 1 mod 2, and has k-dilation at most Œõ, then the same inequality holds: Œõ V_{(M,g)}(O a(k,M)) ‚â• V_{(N,h)}(O a(k,N)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: The key identity is naturality of the fundamental cohomology class under degree-one maps: the induced map on cycle spaces Z_k(Œ¶) satisfies Z_k(Œ¶)^*a(k,N)=(deg Œ¶)a(k,M), hence equals a(k,M) mod 2 when deg Œ¶=1. This follows directly from the gluing definition: glue a family of cycles in M, push the glued (k+i)-chains forward by Œ¶, and the top-dimensional glued class is pushed forward by the degree-one map to the fundamental class of N. Natural cohomology operations commute with pullback, so Z_k(Œ¶)^*(O a(k,N))=O a(k,M). Now choose, for arbitrary Œ¥>0, a family F in M detecting Oa(k,M) with maximal k-volume at most V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M))+Œ¥. Pushing every cycle forward by Œ¶ gives a family in N detecting Oa(k,N), and the k-dilation hypothesis bounds every pushed-forward k-volume by Œõ times the original volume. Therefore V_{(N,h)}(Oa(k,N))‚â§Œõ(V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M))+Œ¥), and Œ¥‚Üí0 gives the result. In the relative U‚äÇM version, first restrict cycles of the family in M to relative cycles in U; the restriction map sends a(k,U) back to a(k,M) because restricting a glued fundamental cycle of M to U gives the relative fundamental class of (U,‚àÇU). Then push forward by Œ¶ and apply the same dilation estimate.

lemmas.label: Theorems 1 and 2: minimax bounds for cup powers and Steenrod towers in the unit ball
lemmas.statement: Let Z(k,n) be the space of mod-2 relative k-cycles in the unit n-ball and let a(k,n)‚ààH^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2) be the fundamental cohomology class. Theorem 1 states that there are constants 0<c(n)<C(n) such that for every positive integer p, c(n) p^{(n-k)/n} ‚â§ V(a(k,n)^p) ‚â§ C(n) p^{(n-k)/n}. For Steenrod squares, write Sq_i: H^N(X;Z2)‚ÜíH^{2N-i}(X;Z2) for the lower-index operation Sq_i(Œ±)=Sq^{N-i}(Œ±), and define iterates Sq_i^Q by repeated application. Theorem 2 states that for every Œµ>0 there is c(n,Œµ)>0 and there is C(n), independent of Œµ, such that for all nonnegative integers Q_0,...,Q_{n-k-1}, c(n,Œµ) ŒÝ_{i=0}^{n-k-1} (2‚àíŒµ)^{((n-k-i)/(n-i)) Q_i} ‚â§ V(Sq_0^{Q_0} Sq_1^{Q_1} ... Sq_{n-k-1}^{Q_{n-k-1}} a(k,n)) ‚â§ C(n) ŒÝ_{i=0}^{n-k-1} 2^{((n-k-i)/(n-i)) Q_i}.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Lower bound for cup powers: introduce V_+, where V_+(Œ±)‚â•V means that in every family F:X‚ÜíZ(k,n), F^*Œ± vanishes on a neighborhood of the set of cycles of volume ‚â§V. Gromov's filling argument gives V_+(a(k,n))‚â•c(n): if all cycles have tiny filling volume, the complex of cycles can be filled over X√ó[0,1] to the zero family, so it cannot sweep out. For a^p, place p disjoint balls B_i of radius r‚âàp^{-1/n}. Let S_i be cycles whose restriction to B_i has k-volume ‚â§const¬∑r^k. By scaling and V_+(a)>0, a vanishes near S_i. Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory says a^p vanishes near ‚à™S_i. Thus a family detecting a^p contains a cycle outside all S_i, with volume at least p¬∑const¬∑r^k‚âàp^{(n-k)/n}. A more general cup product theorem optimizes unequal radii and gives the same mechanism. Lower bound for Steenrod towers: replace LS theory by the paper's Vanishing Lemma. If œÄ:S^i√óX‚ÜíX and œÄ^*Œ± vanishes on an open V‚äÇS^i√óX, then Sq_i Œ± vanishes on P[V]=‚à©_{Œ∏‚ààS^i}(V(Œ∏)‚à™V(‚àíŒ∏)). Iterating this over an i-pyramid of convex subsets of B^n converts smallness of cycle pieces in many convex sets into vanishing of Sq_i^PŒ±. The pyramids are built by repeated equal-volume bisection by hyperplanes whose normals form S^i-families with any n‚àíi consecutive normals orthogonal. The hard geometric estimate controls the pyramid thickness: along the worst bisection tree, estimate Œ£ Rad(C_I)^{-1} using N_Œ≤(C)=sup_a Œ≤^a Vol(C)^{-1/a}œÄ_{n-a}(C)^{1/a}, where œÄ_q is average q-dimensional projection volume. If N_Œ≤ is attained at a, then the approximating rectangle has R_{a+1}‚â´R_a, so after n‚àíi orthogonal cuts the relevant projected volumes grow by at most (2+Œµ)^a. Blocking the tree gives thickness ‚â•c(n,Œµ)(2‚àíŒµ)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P}. Iterating over i and using V_+(a)>0 proves the stated lower bound. Upper bounds: first build detecting families of 0-cycles. For n‚àík=1 use roots of real polynomials; for n‚àík‚â•2 use symmetric products SP^p S^{n-k} and Nakaoka's cohomology computation. Suspend k times to obtain families of unions of parallel k-planes detecting the desired classes. For Steenrod towers, use translation/suspension and addition on symmetric products to model the Steenrod operations. Then apply bend-and-cancel: after a generic rotation, a PL skeleton-squeezing map sends most of each plane into a lattice k-skeleton, with only O(1) bad pieces per plane; mod-2 overlap cancellation leaves volume bounded by skeleton volume plus exceptional pieces. For Steenrod towers use a multiscale sequence of skeleton-squeezing maps, producing exactly the product of powers in the upper bound.

papers.title: Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles
papers.why_relevant: Contains rectangle dilation estimates, the relative isoperimetric profile for rectangles, and cycle-tightening/null-homotopy arguments that are directly useful for proving the needed anisotropic filling lemma.

lemmas.label: Theorem 3
lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with 0<R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n. For a relative integral k-cycle z in (R,‚àÇR), let FV_R(z) be the infimum of the masses of relative integral (k+1)-chains y in R with ‚àÇy=z, and define I_R^k(V)=sup{FV_R(z): z is a relative integral k-cycle in R with mass |z|‚â§V}. For each n there are constants c(n)>0 and C(n)<‚àû such that, for every 1‚â§k‚â§n‚àí1, the following hold. If V‚â§c(n)R_1‚ãØR_k and V is written in the form V=c(n)R_1‚ãØR_j œÅ^{k‚àíj} for some j‚àà{0,...,k‚àí1}, with empty product R_1‚ãØR_0=1, where 0<œÅ‚â§R_1 when j=0 and R_j‚â§œÅ‚â§R_{j+1} when j‚â•1, then I_R^k(V)‚â§C(n)R_1‚ãØR_j œÅ^{k‚àíj+1}. Without any smallness assumption on V, I_R^k(V)‚â§C(n)R_{k+1}V. Consequently, with possibly different dimensional constants c,C, if z is a relative p-cycle in R, 1‚â§j‚â§p‚â§n‚àí1, and |z|‚â§c(n)R_1‚ãØR_j R_j^{p‚àíj}, then z bounds a relative (p+1)-chain in R of mass at most C(n)R_j|z|.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is an induction on k using the Federer-Fleming deformation theorem adapted to a rectangular lattice. The deformation lemma says: for a relative k-cycle z and a rectangular lattice in R with all cell side lengths ‚àºL‚â§R_1 and ‚àÇR in the (n‚àí1)-skeleton, z is homologous to a relative k-cycle z‚Ä≤ in the k-skeleton, with |z‚Ä≤|‚â§C|z| and homology mass ‚â§CL|z|. It is proved by pushing z successively from the p-skeleton to the (p‚àí1)-skeleton: in each p-face choose a random center and radially project to the boundary; average stretching is O_n(1), and the swept homology costs O_n(L) times the mass. Base k=0: a weighted sum of points is filled by joining each point to the face x_1=0, costing ‚â§R_1|z|. Inductive step, small-volume case V‚â§cR_1^k: take L‚âàV^{1/k}‚â§R_1. After deformation, |z‚Ä≤|< one k-cell mass, so integrality forces z‚Ä≤=0; the deformation homology fills z with mass O(LV)=O(V^{(k+1)/k}), which is the j=0 profile. Large case V‚â•cR_1^k: take L=R_1 and choose the lattice so each lattice vertex has x_1-coordinate 0 or R_1. Then every interior k-face contains the whole [0,R_1] factor, so the deformed cycle has product form z‚Ä≤=[0,R_1]√óz_1 in the (n‚àí1)-rectangle [0,R_2]√ó...√ó[0,R_n], with |z_1|‚â§CV/R_1. Apply the induction hypothesis to z_1. If V=cR_1‚ãØR_jœÅ^{k‚àíj}, then z_1 has the corresponding profile with index j‚àí1 and the same œÅ, giving a filling C_1 of mass ‚â§CR_2‚ãØR_jœÅ^{k‚àíj+1}; multiplying by [0,R_1] gives ‚â§CR_1‚ãØR_jœÅ^{k‚àíj+1}. The deformation homology costs ‚â§CR_1V, which is ‚â§CR_1‚ãØR_jœÅ^{k‚àíj+1} since œÅ‚â•R_1 in this case. For the global bound, induction gives z_1 a filling of mass ‚â§CR_{k+1}V/R_1; multiplying by R_1 and adding the deformation cost CR_1V‚â§CR_{k+1}V proves I_R^k(V)‚â§CR_{k+1}V.

lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1
lemmas.statement: Let S=[0,S_1]√ó...√ó[0,S_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with 0<S_1‚â§...‚â§S_n. A complex of cycles in S parametrized by a finite oriented polyhedral complex X means a chain map C from the cellular chain complex of X to the complex I_rel(S) of relative integral Lipschitz chains in (S,‚àÇS), so that each p-face F^p is assigned a relative p-chain C(F^p) and boundaries are respected. There is a constant c(n)>0 such that if C_0 is a complex of cycles in S parametrized by X, C_0(v)=0 for every vertex v of X, and |C_0(F^p)|‚â§c(n)S_1‚ãØS_p for every p-face F^p of X with 1‚â§p‚â§n, then C_0 is null-homotopic: there exists a complex of cycles on X√ó[0,1] restricting to C_0 on X√ó{0} and to the zero complex on X√ó{1}. The same proof applies to complexes parametrized by a pair such as (R,‚àÇR), giving a homotopy parametrized by (R√ó[0,1],‚àÇR√ó[0,1]).
lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct the homotopy C on X√ó[0,1] skeleton by skeleton. Set the target endpoint to the zero complex. Since C_0(v)=0, define C(v√ó[0,1])=0 for each vertex, starting the induction. Assume C has been defined on the (p‚àí1)-skeleton of X√ó[0,1] with the intended mass bounds. For a p-face E of X√ó[0,1], the already-defined C(‚àÇE) is a relative (p‚àí1)-cycle in S. After taking X simplicial or absorbing the bounded number of facets of each face into constants, its mass is ‚â§c‚Ä≤(n)S_1‚ãØS_{p‚àí1}. Choose the initial c(n) small enough so Theorem 3 applies in the easy small-profile range; C(‚àÇE) then bounds a relative p-chain of mass ‚â§c(n)S_1‚ãØS_p. Define C(E) to be such a filling. This inductively extends C through the n-skeleton while preserving smallness. For an (n+1)-face, the boundary is a relative n-cycle of mass <S_1‚ãØS_n if c(n) is small. A nonzero class in H_n(S,‚àÇS;Z) is an integer multiple of the fundamental class and has mass at least Vol(S)=S_1‚ãØS_n, so this n-cycle is exact; fill it arbitrarily. For all higher-dimensional faces there is no obstruction because H_q(S,‚àÇS;Z)=0 for q‚â•n+1. Thus the homotopy to zero is completed.

lemmas.label: Estimate 1
lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] and S=[0,S_1]√ó...√ó[0,S_n] be n-dimensional rectangles with 0<R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n and 0<S_1‚â§...‚â§S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. For each n there is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Suppose U is an open subset of R and Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)‚Üí(S,‚àÇS) is a smooth map of pairs of positive degree D. Then for every choice of integers j,k,l satisfying 0‚â§j<k‚â§l‚â§n, the k-dilation of Œ¶ obeys Dil_k(Œ¶)‚â•c(n) Q_1‚ãØQ_j (Q_{j+1}‚ãØQ_l)^{(k‚àíj)/(l‚àíj)}, with the empty product interpreted as 1. Equivalently, after changing the dimensional constant, if Œ¶ is k-contracting and has nonzero degree, then for all 0‚â§j<k‚â§l‚â§n one has (R_1‚ãØR_j)^{(l‚àíj)/(k‚àíj)} R_{j+1}‚ãØR_l ‚â• c(n)(S_1‚ãØS_j)^{(l‚àíj)/(k‚àíj)} S_{j+1}‚ãØS_l.
lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling, prove the equivalent k-contracting inequalities. For j=0, decompose R into blocks R_1√ó...√óR_k√óŒµ√ó...√óŒµ. The associated complex C_Œ¶ sends p<k faces to zero, sends k-faces to chains of mass ‚â§R_1‚ãØR_k, and sends p>k faces to chains of mass ‚â§R_1‚ãØR_k Œµ^{p‚àík}, using the linear-algebra fact that k-contraction implies p-contraction for p‚â•k. If R_1‚ãØR_k<cS_1‚ãØS_k and Œµ is small, Lemma 4.1 makes C_Œ¶ null-homotopic, contradicting nonzero degree. Applying this to l-dilation and using Dil_l^{k/l}‚â§Dil_k gives all j=0 cases. For 0<j<k<l, Section 5 reduces to the case L‚â§R_{j+1}, where L is defined by R_1‚ãØR_j L^{k‚àíj}=Œ¥(n)S_1‚ãØS_j S_j^{k‚àíj}: for fixed l the desired inequality can be written with right side independent of j, so choose j minimizing the left side; Proposition 4.1 handles j=k‚àí1, and comparison with j+1 gives L‚â§R_{j+1} otherwise. Subdivide R into blocks of dimensions R_1√ó...√óR_j√óL√ó...√óL, and let B be the complex of interior faces. Let C_0(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U). Build a tightened complex C_1 agreeing with C_0 on the k-skeleton. Inductively, if C_1 is defined through dimension p, then for each (p+1)-face the boundary C_1(‚àÇF) has mass ‚â§O(Œ¥ S_1‚ãØS_j S_j^{p‚àíj}); Theorem 3 fills it by a (p+1)-chain of mass ‚â§O(Œ¥ S_1‚ãØS_j S_j^{p+1‚àíj}). The key point is deg C_1=deg C_0=D. This is proved by gradual tightening on dyadic refinements B_s with mesh 2^{‚àís}L. Let Œì_s be the tightened complex on B_s. The glued Œì_{s+1} and Œì_s agree on the k-skeleton and have all higher faces small, so Lemma 6.1, a two-complex version of Lemma 4.1, homotopes them. For large s, the actual image complex Œ≤_s(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U) has higher-dimensional face masses tiny and is homotopic to Œì_s; Œ≤_s has degree D. Hence C_1 has degree D. Now glue C_1 over the coarser subdivision with blocks R_1√ó...√óR_l√óL√ó...√óL. A p-face in the coarse complex is a union of N=(R_{j+1}/L)‚ãØ(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so |C_1^+(F^p)|‚â§CŒ¥ N S_1‚ãØS_j S_j^{p‚àíj}=C(Œ¥)[(R_1‚ãØR_j)/(S_1‚ãØS_j)]^{(l‚àík)/(k‚àíj)} R_1‚ãØR_l S_j^{p‚àíl}. Since C_1^+ has nonzero degree, Lemma 4.1 implies some p-face has mass ‚â•cS_1‚ãØS_p. Comparing and using S_{l+1},...,S_p‚â•S_j gives [(R_1‚ãØR_j)/(S_1‚ãØS_j)]^{(l‚àík)/(k‚àíj)}R_1‚ãØR_l‚â•cS_1‚ãØS_l, equivalent to the stated estimate.
